scientificExpertise: Business and international management; Transport Economics
scientificBackground:
My core research interests include:
1.	Port Economics, Management and Policy
2.	Socio-economic impact of Ports and Shipping on port cities (I have received funding for research by Greek Port Authorities - Thessaloniki Heraklion,)
3.	Circular Economy, Ports, and Shipping (I recently completed a project for Circular Flanders, Belgium) 
4.	Energy transition in ports 
5.	European Policies for Ports (previous funding from successive Jean Monnet Actions in European Port Policy (2003-2018)) 

 The relationship between ports and the port city is a core dimension of port studies. I am interested in changes in maritime transport and how the spatial dimensions of port infrastructures significantly influence and/or are influenced by the port-city interface. These interests and objectives are significant and highly relevant in energy transition and port city development.
motivation: The COST Action will provide the opportunity to study the ways Port City Territory (PCT) is affected by the spatial and functional expansion of the port (the so-called ‘port regionalization), and vice versa, the interplay of the port and the hinterland that is economically dependent on the port, the broader range of the connections and flows that emerge when planning and managing ports. A second dimension is research related to the implementation of circular economy principles in ports and shipping and, as part of it, in (the management of) the energy transition of ports.  A third dimension is the study of stakeholder management, i.e., how the involvement of authorities, city governments, NGOs, private companies, research institutions, local communities, and other state and non-state stakeholders could steer the energy transition. These dimensions will enrich existing research and publications on multi-level governance of ports and maritime-related studies and policies/politics for ports.
contribution:
WG-1. Based on my long experience, I intend to contribute to a coherent and organized implementation, i.e., transmitting ideas, innovation, and themes and publication strategies in my home country and beyond. Proper administration is part of this task.
WG-2. Research in the field needs a better understanding of existing data but also proposals on data gaps and worthy adjustments. I will contribute to revealing the state-of-the-art and what is worth to be done by ports, cities, and stakeholders.
WG-3. Will contribute to discussions on the existing state of theoretical knowledge and initiatives to develop and examine new concepts.
WG-5. Facilitate the development of a dissemination strategy – perhaps taking initiatives towards that, but also orchestrating themes and research to be widely circulated and outputs via which they would be best circulated to the academia and all stakeholders

affiliation: Centre for Contemporary and Digital History, University of Luxembourg
scientificExpertise: Modern and contemporary history
scientificBackground: My academic background includes a BA in Global History and English Literature (University of Zurich) and an MA in History and Philosophy of Knowledge (ETH Zurich). In September 2022, I began my PhD at the Luxembourg Centre for Contemporary and Digital History (C²DH). My research focuses on the development of public urban green spaces in Hamburg and Marseille from 1945 to the 1970s, examining park planning during a period of major urban reconfiguration, economic upswing and decolonization. The project considers the unique attributes of port cities: the porous interface between land and sea, migration, exchange of goods and ideas. I apply theories from spatial, planning, port city, and (trans-)urban history, as well as human geography. Geographic Information Systems (GIS) is my primary analytical tool. I am a member of the Digital History Network Switzerland and PortCityFutures. From September 2024, I will join the editing team for the PortCityFutures blog.
motivation:
Studying port city development from a planning history perspective, I am excited to apply this knowledge to make PCTs more livable and sustainable now and for the future through interdisciplinary collaboration within PACT. My research on Hamburg and Marseille reveals ongoing challenges in public housing, social inclusion, and access to public spaces. These issues are intensifying with population growth and climate change. My findings align with PACT's argument that PCTs and their public spaces suffer from insufficient stakeholder collaboration in planning processes. I am motivated to help connect previously unconnected actors and ensure public interests are heard.
Academic research can often be isolated, with specialists discussing mainly among themselves. I believe research should be applicable and contribute to current issues. By participating in PACT, I hope to bridge the gap between historical analysis and contemporary urban challenges, for more inclusive and sustainable planning processes in PCTs.
contribution:
My work explores environmental justice, class, and gender issues in urban planning through a historical lens. By examining Hamburg and Marseille, I study how past planning decisions continue to impact these port cities today. My analysis on why urban planners and officials made certain choices, often without including the voices of local inhabitants, can inform frameworks for PCT development.
GIS is a powerful tool to visualize power relations, developments over time, and socio-spatial dynamics. My aim is to contribute to PACT by applying GIS to make the complex realities of PCTs accessible to a wider audience and reveal mechanisms of socio-spatial segregation and their long-term effects.
I wish to apply my historical toolset to contemporary challenges, fostering more inclusive and diverse planning processes in PCTs. In addition, specializing in the development of public parks, key in increasing people’s well-being, I can provide knowledge on their planning, use, and necessity.

scientificBackground:
I'm a sociologist who moved towards spatial planning and urban dynamics early in my career, always focusing on the social component of these issues. The complexity of change processes has always been central to my work, interests and research. I have continuously invested in social impact assessment and strategic environmental assessment of change processes affecting territories and communities (and lately in the Portuguese energy system), as can be seen from my curriculum vitae.
For the last seven years, I have focused on the social dimension of energy transitions, especially in the context of climate change mitigation and adaptation strategies, in the context of research and knowledge transfer. Finally, I would like to highlight the approaches I have made on several occasions to the urban and territorial dynamics present in port contexts, with specific characteristics and challenges that become particularly relevant in times of energy transition like those we are experiencing.
motivation: This COST Action - due to its theme and the variety of pilot cases presented - is particularly motivating for me because it fits with my research interests and applied work, but also with my most direct geographical context, where I feel I can have a very close perception of the challenges and opportunities facing port areas in the context of climate change and energy transition. On the other hand, the proposed approach, namely the recognition that 'more than technological responses […] This transition necessitates a value-based approach and collaborative, multi-disciplinary action’, is in line with my conviction that change is crucially based on action focused on soft factors, governance and collaborative, multidisciplinary approaches. The urgency of climate action often leads us to believe that technology and the regulatory framework for energy transition are the key, but without those factors' support, the challenges will be significantly harder to overcome.
contribution: After analysing the Memorandum presented, in particular the objectives and tasks proposed for each of the Working Groups (WGs), I believe I could make a more valuable contribution to the WG1 (PACT Framework), although I could also do so in the WG3 (Theoretical Knowledge). My research and transfer of knowledge on climate change response over the past decade has been substantial. This experience, combined with my work in the Strategic Environmental Assessment of the Portuguese energy sector keeps me well informed of the latest theoretical frame on the issue and correlated issues. It also gives me knowledge about the actors in the system, the role of communities and the impacts on them. This background would enable me to effectively contribute to the tasks outlined for both WG1 and WG3.

WG1) Tracing the state-of-the-art is the first step in both PACT and INTeLOG projects, and my work on NAPA could shed light on the effective multi-level governance of ports and territories in Italy.
WG2) In line with WG2, INTeLOG methodology is based on mapping and spatial analysis (see Ramondetti, 2024). Participating in WG2 will give the opportunity to share expertise, and discuss limits and possibilities of this approach.
WG3) I will contribute to WG3 sharing my theoretical knowledge with PACT’s experts to nurture discussion on the sustainability of ports’ energy and logistics projects.
WG4) Although PACT and INTeLOG explore different case studies, my participation in WG4 will offer complementary insights, providing materials for further comparative analyses.
WG5) My participation in WG5 will help to maximise the dissemination of the research findings by reaching also the academic and general audience targeted by INTeLOG.

scientificExpertise: Risk assessment, prevention and mitigation; Social policies, welfare state; Socio-economic aspects of environmental sciences
scientificBackground: I am a post-doctoral fellow, who focuses on risk perception analysis in the major Lithuanian cities, one of them being the port city (Klaipėda). I think I could contribute to knowledge creation (WP2 and WP3 mainly) since I am capable of conducting scientific and spatial analysis. 
Additionally, I am curious about port cities due to their uniqueness amongst other cities. It seems that having a port in the city changes usual material flows, establishes a different cultural landscape and, most importantly, human perception and use of the city. It would be great to investigate these questions more in-depth via participation in the project.
contribution: I could contribute to WP1 & WP2 & WP3 most significantly by conducting literature reviews, data analysis, theory creation as well as scientific knowledge representation in conferences or articles. My contribution to WP4 & WP5 is limited due to the languages used in the context of case studies (Dutch, French, Italian, Spanish).  However, in tandem with the person, who speaks these national languages, I will contribute happily as much as I can.

affiliation: University of Applied Sciences in Ferizaj
scientificExpertise: Transport engineering
scientificBackground: I have a PhD in Civil Engineering, specializing in Transportation Engineering. My doctoral research focused on modeling air pollution based on driver behavior, providing insights into the complex interactions between human activity and environmental impacts. This research contributes to understanding the behavioral and operational factors influencing emissions, a critical aspect in addressing climate change and sustainability. The critical intersections of transportation, environmental sustainability, and urban development are at the interest of my academic and professional endeavors, and I am eager to contribute my expertise to this project.
motivation: This project is particularly exciting for me because it addresses these multidimensional challenges in a holistic manner. My motivation stems from the need to contribute to a sustainable future through a collaborative, multi-disciplinary approach that bridges the gap between technological advancements and societal needs. As someone who has researched the environmental impacts of transportation systems, I am keen to explore how these insights can be applied to port city territories, where transport, energy, and environmental concerns converge.
contribution: My background in modeling air pollution from driver behavior equips me with the skills necessary to assess the environmental impacts of human activity within complex systems. I believe this expertise can contribute significantly to the PACT project, particularly in understanding and addressing the air quality and emissions challenges faced by port cities during the energy transition. Furthermore, my background in transportation systems can help inform the development of new strategies for integrating sustainable transportation methods into port infrastructure.

scientificExpertise: Port-city relationships, waterfront redevelopment
scientificBackground: I am a spatial planner by training, with a PhD in sociology. My research focuses on port-city relationships, waterfront redevelopment, cultural and sporting mega events in port cities, port city cultures and maritime heritage. I have recently authored a research monograph on cultural and sporting events in port cities, and the mutual connections between such events and the maritime character of port cities. Other publications I have authored focus on the contestation of local meanings in culture-led urban regeneration in port cities, the ‘port city stigma’ affecting declining industrial port cities in the UK, the cultural demaritimisation and remaritimisation of port cities, the emergence of maritime cultural quarters.
motivation: I am eager to contribute to the project to explore the complexity of the energy transition across port cities, as I do believe that such a structural process is likely to produce impacts similar in magnitude to those of deindustrialisation in the 20th century, and therefore to impact all aspects of port city studies. The broader impacts of energy transition is likely to re-define the basic ideas behind the regeneration of urban port areas, and the relationships between ports and cities. I would like to be part of such a paradigm shift for our research area.
contribution: I feel that with my expertise and research interests I can positively contribute to the Theoretical Knowledge WG, in particular in relation to the common glossary and concepts. My profile is highly interdisciplinary, at the crossroads of urban planning, urban geography, marine/maritime sociology. I can contribute in particular to the concepts and glossary related to the 'city side' of port-city relationships, such as the social and cultural aspects of the energy transition, the re-definition of the concept of urban heritage in relation to the restructuring of ports.

contribution:
I am interested in participating in WG3: Theoretical knowledge and WG4: Pilots
I am interested in contributing to the development of common taxonomy and glossary that considers different disciplines and stakeholders of port city territories. Within this COST action I am also interested in how the knowledge and experiences from the selected pilot studies can be exchanged in other contexts facilitating just energy transition.

scientificExpertise: Arhitecture and city professions
scientificBackground: As Dr. Arch, lecturer at Skikda university, Algeria, researcher at PUViT laboratory, Sétif University, and international member of the LDE PortCityFuturs initiative, I have been interested in port cities, and in blue strategies that aim to bring together the various stakeholders and actors involved in the development of port/city interfaces, in terms of sustainable development. In addition, I have been attracted by case studies of energy ports specializing in oil and hydrocarbon functions, and their significant impact on the city, and the environment, leading our research to explore the energy transition issues at the port/cities interface, which is deeply implied in the Action's aims, that is at the heart of my research interests, and will be a valuable opportunity to share my knowledge and expertise. I commit myself to actively participate in network meetings and to produce efficient working papers and scientific collaborations in line with COAT's objectives.
motivation:
I am highly motivated to join the COST PACT Action, as I am extremely interested in its innovative approach, which focuses on energy transition issues in such complex interfaces as the port/ city interface.
My research in this field clearly fits in with COST PACT's objectives, which are to bring a new, more sustainable and resilient breath to port cities, and more particularly to those equipped with oil-energy ports. This represents a major challenge for all the actors and stakeholders involved or living at this critical and vulnerable interface, which precisely requires new and effective visions. In this respect, I am delighted to contribute to the development of collaborative methodologies within the framework of the PACT Action.
contribution: COAST offers a precious opportunity to build a multidisciplinary network that actively contributes to the exchange of expertise on the prospects for energy transition in port cities. COAST therefore has a key role to play in building bridges between the various case studies, all of which are characterized by their own features. In this way, COAST will help to open up ports to each other and encourage the sharing of scientific and practical knowledge between experts. Given my many years of interest in ports, and in particular energy ports, my research objectives enthusiastically overlap with those of COAST to produce more effective research and contributions on energy transitions in port cities. This collaborative initiative is absolutely essential to bring together researchers from all over the world to think about the design of the next generation of port/cities interfaces, which will be characterized through energy transitions.

scientificExpertise: Sustainability
scientificBackground: My academic background and research experience focusing on sustainable shipping and ports and my expertise in maritime energy management are directly relevant to the objectives and scope of the COST Action. I have been engaged as a Principal/Key researcher in various European, regional and national research projects in the field of shipping and port decarbonisation (e.g. Transport Canada project dealing with the abatement of GHG emissions at the Canadian ports) and have conducted in-depth case studies focusing on the assessment of the environmental impact of port operations and the development of strategies for the integration of renewable energy into port infrastructures. More specifically, my research has revolved around the investigation of the feasibility of alternative fuels such as ammonia and hydrogen in shipping in addition to the regulatory framework that facilitates the transition to low-carbon energy systems (IMO GHG Strategy and Fit-for-55 package).
motivation: My main motivation to join the COST Action derives from the acknowledgement that multidisciplinary actions and projects involving multiple stakeholder collaboration are essential in order to proceed with the energy transition and sustainable development of ports. Besides the exploration of technical and operational advances for the energy transition of ports and port-cities, research should also address the socio-economic impacts of ports’ energy transition on local communities and global trade. The interdisciplinary nature of the action—combining engineering, energy policy, environmental science, economics and other social studies—focuses exactly on addressing this need for collaboration among industry, policymakers, and port communities in general, in order to implement practical solutions towards the achievement of the energy transition goals.
contribution: My contribution in WG1 will focus on an extensive literature and exploratory review of the practices and strategies that could enable energy transitions in PCTs. The review includes both academic and 'grey' literature to capture the whole spectrum of the developments and advances with regards to the port-city energy transitions. On the basis of the findings, both a SWOT and a PESTEL analysis can be conducted underlining the Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities and Threats associated with different dimensions of the energy transitions in PCTs. For WG3, my contribution will revolve around the composition of the glossary of terms form the point of an academic maritime economist that has deep knowledge and expertise on the technologies and operational practices that are essential for the energy transition of PCTs. With regards to WG5, I will contribute in the dissemination and communication of the work undertaken in the COST Action.

motivation:
I am deeply invested in the evolving role of port cities in the context of sustainable development and energy transition. My professional journey has been characterized by a commitment to exploring innovative approaches that integrate economic, environmental, and social dimensions within the maritime sector. Consequently, I am highly motivated to contribute to the Working Group 'Pilots' .

The pressing challenges posed by climate change and the urgent need to transition from fossil fuels to renewable energy resonate deeply with my research interests. Ports have historically been critical nodes in global supply chains and energy ecosystems, and I believe that leveraging this unique position can facilitate a meaningful transition towards more sustainable practices. My academic work has equipped me with a robust understanding of the complex interdependencies at play in port city territories, where economic vitality, environmental stewardship, and social inclusivity must coalesce.
contribution:
I recognize the importance of fostering partnerships among researchers, practitioners, and local stakeholders to co-create actionable solutions that reflect the nuanced realities of port cities. My experience in conducting interdisciplinary research and engaging with diverse stakeholder groups aligns well with the goals of this Working Group. Additionally, my involvement in pilot programs in various European port cities has provided me with insights into best practices, challenges, and opportunities that can be leveraged for success in implementing the PACT Framework.

 I aim to contribute my expertise in supply chain dynamics and terminal operations, as well as my commitment to fostering inclusive and sustainable practices. I am eager to collaborate on pilot studies, and to share learnings from these experiments to further enrich the collective knowledge base of the Action. I see immense potential in this initiative to inspire other port cities across Europe to embrace innovative, inclusive approaches to energy transition.

scientificExpertise: Political systems and institutions, governance
scientificBackground: After completing my Master's degree in International Public Management (focus: public policy, international affairs, global economy) at Sciences Po Paris, France, I spent four years doing research and policy analysis at the think tank ECDPM (focus: international cooperation, climate  change adaptation & climate justice, gender equality). I am now based at TU Delft and working on impact (methodologies, indicators) as part of the project 'Bauhaus of the Sea Sails', focusing on sustainability transitions, culture and social inclusion in 7 European coastal/port cities (Rotterdam, Antwerp, Hamburg, Venice, Genova, Lisbon, Malmö).
motivation: I am very interested in the governance of  just sustainability transitions, including at the  European and international levels. Ports are globally connected sites that have a role/responsibility in the climate crisis but also the sustainability transition.  My work includes the development of indicators on sustainability and social inclusion which could be adapted to a range of actors, including port-city actors. The emphasis is developing context-sensitive and politically- savvy methodologies and recommendations that can accompany long-term, ambitious, holistic societal transitions. I am interested in testing this methodology in different locations and refining it, so the Pilots might be a good opportunity to do so. We could support the different pilots in their long term planning on impact, with a focus on the energy transition. We could co-develop indicators with them that they can use for planning and accountability.
contribution:
WG 1: share knowledge and work-in-progress about policy change and impact for sustainability transitions, especially with sensitivity to: political / governance dynamics, climate/social justice, contextual/cultural differences and multi-actor collaboration. Insights from the Bauhaus of the Sea Sails project and reflections on the governance barriers/opportunities impacting the project could be shared. The potential of culturally-rooted and value-based approaches for inspiring political change could be discussed.
WG 4: suggestion to use the pilots to test/refine the methodology we are developing in the Bauhaus of the Sea Sails project, especially our 'library of indicators'. This can help plan for long-term sustainability transitions and ensure transitions are also socially inclusive. Indicators can be developed in a bottom-up manner in each location, in a way that serves the needs of local actors. Indicators could also be a tool to address greenwashing by defining and breaking down what 'sustainability'  means and how to measure it.

scientificExpertise: Architecture engineering

The framework and varied expertise from PACT offer a unique opportunity to effectively contribute to the discussions around port territories and understand the potential transformations these areas will undergo locally and globally.

It is important to understand the risks and opportunities of these territories, as well as researching the historical context to abstract lessons and potential guidelines for the future of PCTs. Hence, PCTs mapping and data analysis can help visualize narratives of energy transitions in order to ensure a holistic systemic understanding of port city territories. Furthermore, my PhD research  “Port industrial landscape in times of energy transition” is assessing PCTs denominated “sacrifice zones” in the Chilean context and in comparison to European cases, highlighted by the ongoing energy transition and carbon neutral accords Chile is currently facing. The insights could prove interesting among cases of study and a great contribution to PACT.

scientificExpertise: Anthropology, ethnology, cultural studies
scientificBackground:
I am currently a junior researcher at the Leiden-Delft-Erasmus Centre PortCityFutures, leading a research project studying the outsider perspectives on the development of Rotterdam as a port city. This pilot project is a crucial step toward exploring a humen-centred approach in port city transitions, in particular concerning the growing gaps between the port, the city, and its people. 

My research builds on my MA thesis research, which focused on the resilience and circularity of the cultural sector in Rotterdam. Drawing from my academic background in urban geography (MSc, LSE) and cultural economics (MA, EUR), I aim to continue to study the intersection of cultural and city development. 

My research methodology leans towards qualitative methods, including in-depth interviews, (auto-)ethnography, and archival research. That said, I am keen to expand my skill set and learn more about quantitative methods in the future.
motivation:
My interest in joining the COST Action stems from a strong commitment to integrating arts and culture into social, political, and economic agendas to help shape resilient, inclusive urban futures that respect ecological boundaries. In port cities, a key challenge for both energy transition and broader sustainable development lies in addressing the often-overlooked socio-cultural dimensions of these transformations.

My research centres on the question, "How can shared cultures and the cultural sector become a transformative force in port cities like Rotterdam?” I see culture as a driver of urban resilience and inclusivity, fostering a sustainable future grounded in people and their shared values yet aligning with planetary boundaries and ecological feasibility. 

I believe the PACT COST Action provides a vital platform and network to collaboratively develop a holistic framework—both theoretical and practical—for advancing sustainable energy transitions in port city territories. This is why I would like to join this initiative.
contribution:
I plan to contribute to Working Group 3 by drawing on my interdisciplinary background in urban geography and cultural economics to explore and articulate the socio-cultural dimensions of key terminologies within the glossary. My research on the cultural dynamics of port cities like Rotterdam allows me to help bridge gaps between technical, social, and cultural interpretations of concepts such as “sustainability” and “port.”

I aim to contribute to bringing diverse perspectives—particularly those of cultural practitioners and urban communities—into the glossary’s development. I also hope that my international background, as someone from Hong Kong now based in Rotterdam, will be an asset to the group’s work. This perspective can help capture the nuances of transdisciplinary discussions and elevate diverse voices in shaping a shared taxonomy.

As part of university research projects, I investigate the socio-economic aspects of a just transition in Ida-Virumaa, home to the Port of Sillamäe, a major logistics hub, within the broader energy transition. More details: https://www.etis.ee/CV/Georgi_Hrenov/eng .

affiliation: Ecole Nationale Supérieure d'Architecture de Marseille
scientificExpertise: Territorial and urban planning ; history of planning ; landscape
scientificBackground:
Since 2010, our team at ENSA Marseille has been developing technical, methodological and teaching expertise in cartographic representation of the territory from the metropolitan to the regional scale (www.caue13.fr/atlas-metropolitain). This work is as much about representation as it is about problem-solving, producing knowledge and helping people to think about the project to transform the region and make it more resilient in the face of ongoing climate change. 
The Mélimed project, which we have been coordinating since 2023, has been looking at the resilience of metropolitan areas on the Mediterranean coast through three case studies: the Tangiers-Tetouan region, the Venice lagoon and the Aix-Marseille-Provence metropolitan area (www.melimed.eu). The aim of this strategic partnership, funded by the European Erasmus+ programme, is to develop teaching methods through workshops that open the way to tailor-made solutions enriched by the cross-fertilisation of participants' skills and methods.
motivation:
Our objectives are to reintegrate the long cycles of nature into our thinking about coastal areas, and to recompose the coastline around a certain variable thickness by taking better account of the scale of coastal river catchment areas. These action research experiments in 3 Mediterranean coastal areas have enabled us to identify and measure both generic and specific phenomena (thickness, uses and practices, changes in the coastline, services, energy transport, etc.). We have analysed these situations in relation to the following themes: water (rivers and coastline), housing and agriculture, and the decarbonisation of energy and transport.
We very much appreciate the work resulting from the research carried out by C. Hein and his team, which has influenced the latest developments in our own research, and we would therefore like to join the PACT project, which we feel is very interesting and in line with our recent research.
contribution: We are proposing to contribute to the ‘knowledge discovery in data’ WG in order to use our experience of research through drawing to provide the group with a case study, that of the Grand Port Maritime de Marseille (GPMM). Our previous cartographic work has provided us with data that enables us to examine the GPMM's two specific situations: the eastern basins and their relationship with the city of Marseille, where they block urban access to the sea, and the western basins and their interfaces with the sensitive natural environments of the Regional park of Camargue. This dual object, with its multi-scalar relationships, from the Mediterranean basin to the more regional scale, and its interfaces, would be a good illustrative example of the port's more or less conflictual relationships with its environment and the challenges it faces in terms of its future development in the face of climate change.

scientificExpertise: Machine Learning; Soft Decision Making
scientificBackground:
Dr. Serdar Enginoğlu is an Associate Professor in the Department of Mathematics at Çanakkale Onsekiz Mart University. He earned his PhD from Tokat Gaziosmanpaşa University under the supervision of Prof. Dr. Naim Çağman, focusing on Soft Matrices. His research specializes in decision-making under uncertainty, machine learning, and data classification. He has developed innovative algorithms such as FPFS-kNN and FPFS-CMC, which dynamically analyze complex datasets using multi-metric frameworks. These methods have demonstrated statistically superior performance in modeling uncertainties, particularly in interdisciplinary applications such as healthcare AI and computational biology.

With over 50 scientific articles, two book chapters, and 24 full-text conference papers, Dr. Enginoğlu has established himself as a leading researcher in algorithmic design. He has actively contributed to 12 national projects, including a current TÜBİTAK-funded study, and mentored 5 PhD and 8 MSc students, fostering the development of early-career researchers. His work bridges mathematics, computer science, and real-world problem-solving.
motivation:
Traditional machine learning methods often lack adaptability in complex, data-scarce environments—a critical challenge in energy transitions for port cities. My expertise in fuzzy parameterized fuzzy soft matrices (fpfs-matrices) addresses these gaps by modeling uncertainties in heterogeneous datasets. The CA23138 PACT initiative provides an ideal platform to apply these innovations to socio-environmental and infrastructural challenges in port city territories. Aligned with WG2 (Knowledge Discovery) and WG5 (Transmitting), I aim to:

Enhance data-driven decision-making for sustainable energy transitions.

Facilitate interdisciplinary collaboration between technical and non-technical stakeholders.
contribution:
Adapting FPFS-kNN and FPFS-CMC to analyze port city datasets (e.g., energy consumption patterns, socio-economic metrics, environmental impacts).
Developing hybrid models integrating machine learning with socio-cultural frameworks to address uncertainties in urban planning.
Leading workshops on machine learning applications for policymakers, urban planners, and early-career researchers.
Designing open-access training materials to share data analysis tools for non-technical stakeholders.
Providing open-source algorithms to support transparent, reproducible research across PACT's pilot studies.
Leverage experience in international projects (e.g., computational biology collaborations) to foster cross-disciplinary partnerships.

Ioannis Katsounis is a Social and Political Scientist. He is a Lecturer and Postdoctoral Researcher at the University of Athens, in the Department of Ports Management and Shipping. He graduated from the Department of Sociology at Panteion University of Social and Political Sciences in 2009 and holds a MSc International and European Studies from the University of Piraeus (2013), as well as a PhD from the University of the Aegean (Department of Shipping, Trade and Transport, 2023).
His research interests focus on Maritime Sociology, the Blue Economy, Social Cohesion, Sustainable Development, and Regional and Island Development. He has participated as a researcher and scientific associate in a significant number of research programs related to strengthening social cohesion and sustainable development in Greece and EU.
He also represents Greece as a MC Member in the COST Action  “Rethinking the Blue Economy: Socio-ecological impacts and opportunities” (RethinkBlue) for the period 2023–2027 (https://rethinkblue.eu/).
motivation: As a Social and Political Scientist with an academic background in Maritime Sociology, the Blue Economy, and Sustainable Development, I am deeply motivated to contribute to WG1 of the PACT. My current  position at the National and Kapodistrian University of Athens, combined with my participation in the “RethinkBlue” COST Action, demonstrates my commitment to interdisciplinary approaches that foster inclusive and socially just transitions in port city regions. My research focuses on the intersection of maritime activities, regional development, and social cohesion, dimensions that are central to the PACT Framework. I am particularly interested in exploring how spatial and cultural diversity in port territories can be better integrated into participatory planning methodologies. I believe my expertise can contribute to the development of adaptable tools that promote stakeholder collaboration, empower local communities, and support sustainable energy transitions. I look forward to actively engaging in the group’s work and sharing best practices.
contribution: I aim to contribute to WG1 by bringing a socio-political and territorial development perspective to the formulation of the PACT Framework. My academic and research work has focused on the socio-spatial dynamics of port cities and island regions, emphasizing inclusive governance, social cohesion, and sustainable development. Drawing from my experience in EU-funded projects and my participation in the COST Action “RethinkBlue,” I can offer insight into participatory processes and community engagement practices tailored to diverse local contexts. I am particularly interested in contributing to the co-design of methodological tools that reflect the cultural and spatial complexity of port territories, especially in island and peripheral areas. My contribution will focus on connecting theory with practice, supporting comparative analysis, and enhancing knowledge exchange between academia, policymakers, and local stakeholders to promote socially just and inclusive energy transitions.

scientificExpertise: International studies, strategic studies, human rights, global and transnational governance; Environmental regulations and climate negotiations (policy and political aspects); History and philosophy of political science
scientificBackground: I am a faculty member at METU focusing on energy transition, geopolitics, and environmental governance in Asia–Europe relations. My Ph.D. examined India’s shift from coal to renewables using qualitative methods, including discourse analysis and case studies. I lead a METU-funded project on Asia Studies in Turkey and co-develop a TÜBİTAK proposal titled “Turkey’s Perception of India and the Multipolar Order.” My research bridges strategic narratives, emission mitigation, and climate policy governance. I teach graduate courses on energy and environmental issues in Asia and regularly engage in international interdisciplinary networks
motivation: I am motivated to join PACT to contribute to inclusive energy transition strategies in port city territories, drawing from my expertise in energy and climate governance and qualitative research. PACT’s focus on socio-cultural, spatial, and justice-centered objectives—mapping, reframing, co-creating, including, envisioning, and transmitting—is directly aligned with my interest in sustainable, people-centred transitions. I believe my regional and academic background can help shape PACT’s participatory methodologies and pilot-based innovations.
contribution: I aim to contribute meaningfully across all PACT working groups by integrating climate policy, energy transition, and qualitative-geopolitical insights. For the PACT Framework, I bring expertise in governance and strategic narratives related to low-carbon transitions. In WG2 (Knowledge discovery in data), I support data mapping from energy and socio-cultural policy perspectives. In WG3 (Theoretical knowledge), I help develop shared ethical language and concept glossaries across disciplines. In WG4 (Pilots), I offer case-study-informed approaches to inclusive planning in port cities. In WG5 (Transmitting), I support dissemination and stakeholder engagement through workshops and community outreach, emphasising inclusion and sustainable justice-based narratives in energy transitions

scientificExpertise: Electro-optics (theory)
scientificBackground: I hold a PhD in Bioinformatics and Artificial Intelligence, with extensive expertise in machine learning, deep learning, biostatistics, and data-driven modeling. My research spans healthcare, sustainability, and education, with a strong focus on applying AI techniques to solve real-world problems. I have published widely on predictive modeling, medical data analysis, and technology adoption, and I lead several international collaborations in Europe and the Mediterranean region. My background combines statistical rigor with interdisciplinary applications, enabling me to contribute both methodological and applied perspectives. This experience is directly relevant to the COST Action, particularly in leveraging AI and data science for sustainable development and user-centered applications.
motivation: I am motivated to join this Working Group because I strongly believe in the potential of Artificial Intelligence to address sustainability challenges through innovative, user-centered applications. My current work focuses on applying AI in healthcare and education, and I see great opportunities to extend these approaches to broader domains, such as energy, environment, and smart services. Being part of this COST Action will allow me to collaborate with leading experts, exchange knowledge across disciplines, and jointly develop impactful applications that bridge scientific advances and societal needs. I am particularly interested in co-developing real use cases with stakeholders, ensuring that AI solutions are ethical, inclusive, and sustainable.
contribution: I will contribute to this Working Group by bringing expertise in AI modeling, statistical analysis, and interdisciplinary research design. Specifically, I can support the identification and development of application use cases, contribute to methodological frameworks for evaluating AI-driven solutions, and provide guidance on data analysis, validation, and ethical considerations. My experience in managing international research projects will also support coordination, knowledge dissemination, and engagement with stakeholders from academia, industry, and policy. Additionally, I can contribute to training activities by preparing materials, workshops, and guidelines that foster capacity-building among early-career researchers. By combining methodological expertise and applied experience, I aim to strengthen the Working Group’s efforts in designing practical and scalable AI applications that advance sustainability goals.

scientificExpertise: Civil engineering
scientificBackground: I study socio-technical drivers of bioenergy uptake, applying ML to rank policy and market determinants beyond pure technology. I have built spatial inventories of organic-waste streams (municipal, food industry, sludge), estimated biomethane and heat potential, and compared options under cost, emissions and reliability metrics. My background in indicator design and policy mining helps interpret institutional constraints and equity impacts. Port territories integrate logistics, industry and communities; the same framework I use for national comparisons can be adapted to map flows across ports and test circular options such as anaerobic digestion of port and urban biowaste, renewable gases for port fleets, and heat recovery that supports nearby districts while respecting local environmental limits.
motivation: Ports condense the energy transition: dense demand, many actors, limited space, sensitive ecosystems. I want to help PACT turn data into shared, practical narratives that authorities and communities can act on. I am motivated to test circular bioenergy and waste solutions that improve air quality and fairness while supporting reliable energy services. PACT’s cycle—mapping, reframing, co-creating, including, envisioning, transmitting—matches how I work: make assumptions explicit, explore trade-offs with stakeholders, and publish reusable tools so others can repeat the analysis in new sites. I aim to connect port strategies with city plans and national targets, so that local pilots scale without losing attention to distributional effects and everyday user experience.
contribution:
1. Mapping: geo-reference organic-waste and residual-heat sources; estimate biomethane and circular-use potential with transparent factors.
2. Co-creating: run focused workshops with port authorities, utilities and communities; capture jobs, air-quality and affordability metrics.
3. Transmitting: produce short scenario notes (cost, emissions, reliability, equity) and simple dashboards; document methods for replication in Le Havre, Livorno, Rotterdam, Cádiz and follower ports.
4. Deliverables: open datasets, clear assumptions, and portable tools that support inclusive, evidence-based transition pathways.

scientificExpertise: Applied mechanics, thermodynamics; Characterization methods of materials for material engineering applications; Mechanical and acoustical properties of condensed matter for materials engineering applications; New materials: oxides, alloys, composite, organic-inorganic hybrid; Biomaterials, metals, ceramics, polymers, composites; Databases, data mining, data curation, computational modelling
scientificBackground: I am a Professor of Mechanical Engineering at Abdullah Gül University with a PhD from Koç University and international research experience at UCLA, Purdue, and Kyushu University. My expertise spans advanced manufacturing, materials science, and solid mechanics, with a focus on hydrogen embrittlement, high-entropy alloys, titanium alloys, and additive manufacturing. I have led research on 3D-printed biomedical implants and functional surfaces designed for extreme environments. This scientific background equips me with strong skills in material durability, process optimization, and sustainable design—key elements for the energy transition in port city territories. My interdisciplinary projects demonstrate my ability to bridge engineering innovations with societal and environmental challenges, aligning closely with the goals of COST Action CA23138.
motivation: My motivation to join COST Action CA23138 is driven by a strong commitment to contributing engineering expertise to inclusive and sustainable energy transitions. Port city territories face complex challenges at the intersection of infrastructure, environment, and society. I am motivated to collaborate with experts across disciplines to explore how advanced manufacturing, new materials, and energy-efficient technologies can support resilient port ecosystems. The Action offers an excellent platform to extend my research into practical, societal applications and to align engineering innovations with policy and governance frameworks. I am especially inspired by the collaborative laboratory model, which fosters cross-border dialogue and ensures that technological advances are developed inclusively, supporting both environmental sustainability and local communities.
contribution: I plan to contribute to the Working Groups by sharing expertise in advanced manufacturing, sustainable materials, and structural durability for energy-related applications. My experience with additive manufacturing and multi-scale modeling can support the development of innovative solutions for infrastructure resilience and energy optimization in port city territories. Additionally, I can contribute to capacity building and training by mentoring early-career researchers in applying engineering methods to sustainability challenges. I am also eager to engage in interdisciplinary collaborations, linking engineering research with urban studies, policy, and social sciences to ensure that technological solutions are inclusive and widely applicable. By participating actively in dissemination and joint research, I aim to strengthen PACT’s mission of advancing collaborative, sustainable energy transitions in European port cities.

scientificExpertise: Sustainable engineering, adaptation to long-term environmental changes
scientificBackground: Currently affiliated with the Department of Civil Engineering at the University of Le Havre, the research focuses on soil, sediment, and groundwater remediation technologies. Another major area of interest is the reuse and recycling of waste materials for sustainable applications in the construction sector.
motivation: We have been working for several years on dredged sediments from Le Havre Harbour, developing innovative techniques for the remediation of contaminated sediments. This project is fully aligned with our expertise and represents an excellent opportunity to contribute and strengthen our research activities. Our main contribution will be to WP3 – Pilot Actions Implementation, by identifying target sediment deposits, performing laboratory remediation tests, and developing valorisation pathways. We will also support WP4 – Monitoring & Replication, through performance assessment and data provision for validation.

We can be considered in 3. Theoretical Knowledge, due to our expertise in soil, sediment, and groundwater remediation, and in the reuse of waste materials in construction. At the same time, our long experience with dredged sediments from Le Havre Harbour qualifies us for 4. Pilots, ensuring both scientific input and practical implementation.

scientificExpertise: Family studies
scientificBackground: Using an ethnographic approach, I am interested on the one hand in the subsistence economy (paid work, domestic work, parental and care work, social benefits: how do people live?) in two working-class neighborhoods in Le Havre, and on the other hand in residents' concerns and knowledge about environmental risks (how will we live?).
motivation: I am very interested in meeting other researchers from all disciplines who are working on environmental issues in the port and city of Le Havre. contribution: My ethnographic approach to family dynamics surrounding daily subsistence and attitudes toward the future will provide the residents' perspective through interviews and observations in the port city of Le Havre.

scientificExpertise: Urban sustainability
scientificBackground: I am a sustainability expert with over a decade of experience in developing and implementing tools, strategies, and partnerships for sustainable urban and territorial development. Holding a Master of Science degree in Environment from the University of Melbourne, my expertise lies in cross-sectoral collaboration, participatory diagnostics, and integrated planning for cities and regions. I led the technical design of the City Scan methodology at the UN Global Compact – Cities Programme, supporting municipalities worldwide in assessing governance, sustainability, and development priorities. More recently, I have coordinated the development of Territorio Azul, a strategy that fosters multi-actor collaboration in water-connected territories, and co-designed initiatives linking environmental, social, and economic agendas. With experience across Europe, Latin America, and Australia, I bring a multicultural, solutions-oriented approach to framing integrative strategies that strengthen resilience and equity in cities and territories.
motivation: I am motivated to join this COST Action because it represents a unique opportunity to connect my passion for place-based sustainability with a collaborative European network. Having lived and worked across different countries, I have witnessed how cities and territories creatively address their challenges, and I strongly believe that port cities—given their strategic role at the crossroads of global and local dynamics—are key to shaping sustainable futures. I am eager to contribute my experience in co-creation, participatory diagnostics, and alliance-building, while also learning from diverse perspectives to enrich my own practice. For me, joining this Action is not only about advancing knowledge, but also about fostering collective capacity to design integrated and innovative solutions that improve people’s quality of life and protect natural environments.
contribution: In Working Group 1, I plan to contribute my expertise in participatory diagnostics, cross-sectoral collaboration, and territorial strategy design. My experience developing the City Scan and Territorio Azul has equipped me with practical tools for linking governance, social, and environmental dimensions into integrated frameworks. I can contribute to the PACT framework by sharing lessons from Europe, Latin America, and Australia, particularly on fostering multi-actor alliances and translating sustainability goals into actionable strategies for port city territories. My contribution will focus on co-creating a framework that is not only conceptually sound but also practical, inclusive, and adaptable to diverse local contexts.

scientificExpertise: Artificial intelligence, intelligent systems, multi agent systems; Educational technolocy; Sustainability
scientificBackground: Dr. Thanasis Hadzilacos is Senior Researcher and Visiting Professor at Frederick University (Maritime Transport and Commerce) with a long track record in system design, governance, and citizen engagement for sustainability. He played a pivotal role in the Lemesos Climate City Contract, which received the EU Cities Mission Label in 2024, and in the Eastern Mediterranean and Middle East Climate Change Initiative. Educated at Harvard (BSc, MA) and Patras (PhD in Database Theory under Prof. Papadimitriou), he has led more than 60 EU and nationally funded R&D projects. His expertise combines data and knowledge management, GIS, and large-scale information systems with participatory governance and educational technology. This positions him strongly for WG1 (design of inclusive frameworks), WG2 (knowledge discovery from multi-source data), and WG4 (application in pilot port-city cases, notably Limassol). His experience in bridging academic, municipal, and citizen perspectives aligns directly with PACT’s collaborative, value-based approach to port–city energy transitions
motivation: As a computer scientist since 1975, I have worked across fields as diverse as medicine, archaeology, and education, always with deep respect for their essence rather than allowing digital technology to overrun them by force or speed. My next focus is the meeting point of shipping and AI: how can we make shipping ecologically and socially sustainable? How can AI make jobs in shipping richer and more engaging rather than replace them? And how can technology support ports in being hubs of peaceful intercultural understanding—as they have been for centuries—rather than merely fast and dirty conduits for goods, or hosts of cruise tourism for shopping instead of for learning through lived experience?
contribution: My contribution to PACT will be shaped by questions—questions that are unsettling, provocative, sometimes disruptive, yet always constructive. Good answers already are now instantly available through AI;  what is often missing are the right questions to open new paths. I bring a long arc of perspective: from decades of experience I see continuity with the past, clarity for the present, and a strong commitment to sustainability for the future. My work has always been interdisciplinary, spanning computer science, education, urban governance, and climate initiatives, which helps me connect technological, social, and cultural dimensions. I also bring an intercultural lens: I have lived and worked in Greece, the USA, Cyprus, Switzerland, and Canada, while my family connects me to the UK, Italy, France, and Australia. This diversity informs a view of ports and shipping not only as infrastructures, but as shared spaces of people, knowledge, and cultures.

scientificExpertise: Architecture
scientificBackground: I have a background in urban design and planning, with significant experience in bringing research insights into practical solutions for cities. My Master’s degree in Urbanism gave me a strong scientific foundation, where I explored the relationship between theory and design and tested how ideas could be applied in practice. This combination of academic training and professional work has helped me understand both the complexity of urban systems and the importance of making research accessible and useful for real-world challenges. I enjoy working at the intersection of theory and practice, translating scientific knowledge into approaches that can improve planning and design outcomes.
motivation: I am motivated to join this COST Action by my interest in how port cities will evolve under the pressures of energy transition and climate change. Ports are unique territories where infrastructures, ecosystems and societies intersect, and where global challenges become tangible. I am particularly interested in how energy systems shape both the physical landscape and the cultural identity of these places, and what this means for their future development. Through my background in urban design and planning, I aim to contribute to rethinking these transitions not only as technological shifts but as opportunities to design more inclusive and sustainable port city futures. PACT offers an inspiring platform to collaborate, exchange knowledge and test innovative approaches.
contribution: As a junior researcher at TU Delft, I am currently working on reactivating the heritage of Amsterdam’s quay walls as a strategy to support sustainable urban development. This research connects spatial design with cultural values and ecological considerations, while addressing pressing challenges of climate adaptation and infrastructure renewal. I plan to bring this expertise to the Working Groups by contributing design-based and spatial perspectives on how heritage and infrastructure can play a role in sustainable energy transitions. In the future, I aim to apply similar approaches to the energy systems of Rotterdam and other harbour cities, exploring how infrastructures can be reimagined to serve both functional and societal goals.

scientificExpertise: Landscape Architecture
scientificBackground: Regina Klinger is a Landscape Architecture professional and Junior Researcher with international experience. She recently graduated cum laude with a Master’s degree in Architecture, Urbanism and Building Sciences (Track Landscape Architecture) from Delft University of Technology, where she also worked as a teaching assistant. She holds a Bachelor’s degree in Landscape Architecture and Planning from Technical University of Munich and has gained practical experience working at landscape architecture firms in Singapore, Zurich, and Munich. Her MSc graduation thesis explored the design of subterranean water systems as landscape infrastructure, aiming to create socio-ecologically inclusive and climate-adaptive public spaces in the historic city center of Naples, Italy.
motivation: My motivation to join PACT comes directly from my current research in the Multifunctional Quay Walls project in Amsterdam. Here, I work on connecting archival sources and historical narratives with geospatial datasets and design practice. This experience has shown me how heritage knowledge is often fragmented or overlooked in engineering and urban planning, even though it contains essential insights for sustainable futures. By joining PACT, I aim to link these local experiences with broader European discussions on data discovery and theoretical frameworks for transformations. I believe Amsterdam can serve as a case study for demonstrating how historical knowledge can be activated in practical design contexts, and I want to contribute to the network by showing how heritage and data integration can support interdisciplinary collaboration and policy development.
contribution: In WG2 and WG3, I would contribute through my work on Amsterdam’s canal district, where archival knowledge, geospatial data, and design practice intersect. The Multifunctional Quay Walls project provides a living case for testing how fragmented historical sources can be translated into structured digital datasets and how these can inform future-oriented design and engineering decisions. My contribution will focus on methods for knowledge discovery that combine archival interpretation with computational analysis and on building theoretical frameworks for digital circularity - reusing historical data in planning sustainable futures. I also aim to share insights from working across disciplines with engineers, urban designers, and heritage professionals, offering PACT a grounded example of how theory and practice can be connected in a UNESCO World Heritage context.

This experience has required bridging legal frameworks such as UNCLOS, IMO treaties, and the EU Green Deal with local implementation strategies, ensuring that global sustainability goals are reflected in port city contexts. I have also collaborated with academic and policy institutions on port-related research, including emissions monitoring and environmental risk assessments. This interdisciplinary expertise allows me to contribute to PACT by linking regulatory, environmental, and societal dimensions of inclusive energy transition in port city territories.
motivation: I am motivated to join PACT because I believe port city territories are at the heart of the energy transition, where environmental, social, and economic challenges converge. My career in maritime and environmental law has shown me how ports can act both as drivers of sustainability and as sources of environmental vulnerability, requiring governance models that balance global frameworks with local realities. I am particularly inspired by PACT’s collaborative and value-based approach, which moves beyond technology to include communities, culture, and inclusive planning. I want to contribute by helping to translate international sustainability objectives into practical strategies for port cities, ensuring that transitions are equitable, resilient, and aligned with broader climate and societal goals.
contribution: I can contribute to the Working Group on the PACT Framework by bringing expertise in maritime and environmental law, with a focus on translating international sustainability goals into actionable strategies for port cities. My professional background includes advising governments, the EU, and international organizations on decarbonization, pollution prevention, and integrating green technologies into maritime operations. I have also worked on projects that assess emissions and environmental risks in port areas, ensuring compliance with global frameworks such as MARPOL and the EU Green Deal. Within the PACT Framework, I can help map regulatory and policy dimensions, contribute to co-creation processes with stakeholders, and support the development of inclusive strategies that align legal, environmental, and social aspects of energy transition in port city territories.

scientificExpertise: Quantitative methods for the social sciences
scientificBackground: Port city territories have historically served as key nodes for global energy flows, deeply integrated into fossil-fuel-based economies. In the era of climate crisis and energy transition, these areas stand at the frontline of both logistical transformation and socio-economic restructuring. The COST Action CA23138 – PACT aims to re-examine port–city interfaces through spatial, cultural, and social perspectives to foster inclusive and sustainable energy transitions. Developing interdisciplinary methodologies that support the transformation of port territories toward resilient and low-carbon systems is therefore essential. My academic background focuses on port–city integration, energy planning, and the resilience of urban systems, with a particular emphasis on modelling, co-production processes, and participatory planning approaches. This expertise aligns strongly with PACT’s main axes of “mapping, co-creating, including, and transmitting,” offering valuable contributions to the collaborative development of strategies for sustainable and equitable port city energy transitions.
motivation: My motivation to join the PACT Action arises from a strong interest in understanding how port city territories can adapt to the global energy transition while maintaining economic vitality and social inclusiveness. I believe that digitalisation, data-driven decision-making, and sustainable logistics play crucial roles in shaping low-carbon port ecosystems. Through my doctoral research on digital competitiveness in container transportation, I have explored the interaction between technological innovation and environmental performance in maritime systems. Participating in PACT would provide an exceptional opportunity to collaborate with researchers from diverse disciplines and contribute analytical perspectives to mapping and modelling the transition pathways of port cities. I am particularly inspired by the Action’s interdisciplinary and collaborative spirit, which aligns with my academic vision of fostering sustainable, resilient, and digitally integrated maritime and urban environments.
contribution: Within the PACT Framework working group, I aim to contribute by integrating my expertise in maritime logistics and digital transformation into analytical models that explain the dynamics of port city energy transitions. My research experience in system modelling and sustainability assessment can support the development of frameworks that link digitalisation, logistics efficiency, and low-carbon strategies in port territories. In the Knowledge Discovery in Data working group, I plan to apply bibliometric and data-driven analysis methods to map research trends on decarbonisation, port-city integration, and energy transition. These studies will help identify knowledge clusters, emerging topics, and collaboration networks that strengthen the Action’s research base. For the Theoretical Knowledge working group, I aim to contribute to conceptual discussions on how digitalisation, network theory, and system thinking can explain structural transformations in maritime and urban systems, fostering interdisciplinary dialogue toward inclusive and sustainable port city development.

scientificExpertise: Cultural heritage, cultural memory
scientificBackground: Port city territories (PCTs) are complex socio-spatial interfaces where maritime, ecological, and urban systems converge. Historically central to energy transport and transformation, these areas now face the urgent challenge of transitioning from fossil fuels to renewable sources. Yet, fragmented governance and siloed knowledge hinder coordinated action. Existing research explores port-city evolution and climate adaptation, but lacks integrated, place-based frameworks that address energy transitions through spatial, social, and cultural lenses. In line with the European Commission Green Deal and global climate objectives, inclusive and collaborative approaches are essential. COST Action CA23138 (PACT) responds to this gap by developing a shared taxonomy, collaborative planning tools, and pilot case studies in Le Havre, Livorno, Rotterdam, and Cádiz. By fostering transnational knowledge exchange and empowering stakeholders, PACT aims to enable just, sustainable, and spatially sensitive energy transitions in PCTs, contributing to resilient port cities and equitable urban futures.
motivation: My motivation to join COST Action CA23138 stems from a strong professional and academic commitment to advancing inclusive and sustainable energy transitions in port city territories. As a researcher and practitioner working on urban resilience, climate adaptation, and participatory planning, I see great value in contributing to a collaborative, multidisciplinary network that bridges spatial, social, and governance dimensions of energy transition. This Action offers a unique opportunity to co-create new knowledge, develop place-based methodologies, and exchange experiences with leading experts across Europe. I am particularly interested in contributing to stakeholder engagement processes, inclusive governance models, and spatial analysis of port city regions. Participating in COST will enable me to strengthen my research capacities, build long-term collaborations, and support the implementation of fair and effective energy transition strategies at local, national, and European levels.
contribution: I plan to contribute actively to the Working Groups of COST Action CA23138 by bringing expertise in participatory urban planning, climate adaptation, and stakeholder engagement. My focus will be on supporting the development of inclusive governance models and co-creation methodologies that connect local communities, port authorities, and decision-makers. I aim to collaborate particularly with WG2 (mapping and reframing narratives) and WG4 (pilot case studies), contributing to spatial analysis, participatory workshops, and knowledge transfer activities. I can also support WG5 in dissemination by translating complex research outcomes into accessible outputs for policy and practice. By integrating place-based insights from local contexts, I intend to help strengthen the PACT Framework and its application to diverse port city territories. My contribution will emphasize bridging research and practice, promoting collaborative learning, and enhancing the Action’s impact on just and sustainable energy transitions.

scientificBackground:
Dr Umur Bucak is an academic specialising in maritime transport and port management, focusing his research on the sustainability, efficiency and strategic transformation of the maritime sector. Bucak began his academic career in 2012 as a Research Assistant at the Faculty of Maritime Sciences at Zonguldak Bülent Ecevit University and has been serving as an Associate Professor at the Faculty of Maritime Sciences at Kocaeli University since 2023.
In recent years, his published scientific articles have focused on the themes of environmental sustainability and green transformation in the maritime sector. His articles, published in internationally indexed journals such as The International Journal of Logistics Management, WMU Journal of Maritime Affairs, Marine Policy, Case Studies on Transport Policy, International Journal of Shipping and Transport Logistics, and Research in Transportation Business & Management, make significant contributions to the literature on maritime research.

Alongside his research activities, he participates in projects conducted under the TÜBİTAK Scientific Personnel Support Programmes Presidency's 2209-A programme; he is also involved in an international research project entitled ‘ISPS Search Procedures in European Ports’, supported by the Confederation of European Security Services (CoESS).
contribution: My academic background and research experience in maritime business management enable me to examine energy transition in port cities from the perspective of both economic efficiency and environmental sustainability. My work has focused particularly on the decarbonisation of port facilities, alternative fuel strategies, the impact of the EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) on maritime transport, and natural resource-based management models. This body of knowledge aligns with the laboratory's goals of collaborative management and policy design. Consequently, thanks to my expertise in maritime transport and port economics, as well as my research background focused on sustainability, I can contribute to the laboratory's vision for fair, inclusive and collaborative energy transition in port cities through political analysis, strategic planning and the development of decision support systems.

scientificBackground: I am Lecturer Dr. Şakir Mirza I am a lecturer in the Logistics Program of the Management and Organization Department at Eşme Vocational School, Uşak University.
