I completed my doctoral studies in the management and organization department in 2024. My academic work focuses on logistics, management and organization, and organizational behavior.
motivation:
Dear colleagues,
Ports have always been engines of connection, growth, and transformation. Today, they stand at the heart of one of humanity’s greatest challenges — the transition to a sustainable and inclusive energy future. PACT invites us to look beyond infrastructure and technology, to see ports as living laboratories where people, cities, and ecosystems interact.
Our mission is not only to plan but to co-create — to map new possibilities, reframe challenges, and envision futures built on collaboration and shared values. Through our joint efforts in Le Havre, Livorno, Rotterdam, Cádiz, and beyond, we can shape resilient port city regions that balance progress with responsibility.
Together, we can turn ports into symbols of renewal — places where innovation meets inclusion, and where local actions inspire global transformation. Let’s act, learn, and lead — as one united network for change.
contribution:
My contribution to the PACT working group will focus on integrating sustainability, logistics, and socio-economic perspectives into the transformation of port city regions. With my academic background in logistics and supply chain management, I aim to contribute to understanding how energy transitions reshape economic flows, spatial structures, and community relations in port territories.
I plan to actively participate in collaborative research, knowledge co-production, and comparative analysis of port city case studies. By fostering dialogue between researchers, policymakers, and local stakeholders, I hope to strengthen the link between theoretical frameworks and practical implementation.
Furthermore, I will support PACT’s mission of inclusiveness and interdisciplinary cooperation by engaging with early-career researchers and sharing insights on sustainable logistics practices. My goal is to contribute to developing actionable strategies that make port cities more resilient, innovative, and socially inclusive in the face of the global energy transition.

scientificExpertise: Energy aspects of electrical and electronic engineering
scientificBackground: My research background lies in electrical machines and energy systems, with a focus on the design and optimization of sustainable electromechanical energy conversion systems. My work includes developing novel wound field flux-switching machines and modeling their performance for renewable energy applications such as wind generators. This expertise provides a strong technical foundation for understanding the physical and infrastructural aspects of the energy transition in port city territories (PCTs). In parallel, my academic collaborations involve interdisciplinary projects linking engineering innovation with environmental and socio-economic impact assessment. These experiences align with PACT’s objectives of integrating technological, spatial, and societal dimensions toward inclusive and just energy transitions.
motivation: I am motivated to join the COST Action PACT because it bridges technological innovation with inclusive governance and energy justice in port city territories. As an engineer focusing on sustainable energy systems, I see a unique opportunity to contribute technical insight to multidisciplinary discussions on decarbonization and socio-spatial transformation. The Action’s emphasis on collaborative frameworks and participatory approaches resonates with my interest in linking advanced energy technologies to real-world community needs. Participating in this network will expand my understanding of how technological transitions can be embedded in complex territorial and social contexts, supporting both academic growth and the European Green Deal’s objectives.
contribution:
Within WG1 (PACT Framework), I aim to contribute by integrating engineering perspectives into the development of collaborative models for inclusive energy transition in port city territories. My background in energy systems design and optimization enables me to help identify technical parameters and infrastructural challenges relevant to stakeholder cooperation and governance structures.

In WG3 (Taxonomy and Glossary), I plan to support the creation of a shared interdisciplinary vocabulary that connects engineering, spatial planning, and social sciences. I can contribute by clarifying key technical concepts in renewable energy conversion, power systems, and sustainability metrics to ensure coherence across disciplines.

For WG4 (Pilots), I will assist in testing and validating the PACT framework through applied case studies, particularly by linking technical energy solutions with socio-spatial outcomes. My role will focus on evaluating the feasibility and impact of energy transition technologies within the selected port city territories.

scientificExpertise: Civil engineering; Sustainable engineering, adaptation to long-term environmental changes; Health and Safety
scientificBackground: My expertise in Construction Engineering and Sustainable Environmental Management provides a critical technical and socio-economic lens for analyzing the Port-City Territories in Action (PACT) objectives. For 13 years, my academic work has focused on the intersection of large-scale infrastructure, sustainability, and environmental impacts. Crucially, I am based in Zonguldak, a quintessential port city defined by its deep historical reliance on coal mining and thermal power plants. This unique context now faces a dramatic energy transition with the recent Black Sea natural gas discovery. My background is essential for grounding the PACT framework within the realities of fossil fuel dependence and the urgent need for a just transition. My research on construction management and environmental quality directly supports PACT's goal of understanding the complex spatial and corporate dynamics inherent in shifting from conventional to renewable energy hubs.
motivation: I am highly motivated to join PACT to bridge the theoretical work on energy transition with the urgent, real-world context of Zonguldak, a city where the complex problems PACT addresses are intensely condensed. The transition from being Turkey's historic coal heartland to a new energy hub (natural gas) presents a unique case study for mapping, inclusion, and envisioning a sustainable future. I believe my expertise in large-scale infrastructure management and sustainable development is vital for contributing to the Action's value-based approach. Furthermore, my active role in a key regional university will facilitate the necessary knowledge co-production and collaborative planning between the port, city, and local stakeholders. I am committed to leveraging the PACT network to develop a globally relevant, locally applicable, and inclusive energy transition model for Zonguldak that inspires other port cities.
contribution:
I will actively contribute to the Knowledge discovery in data and Transmitting Working Groups, utilizing my academic and regional expertise.

Knowledge Discovery: I will lead the collection and analysis of quantitative and qualitative data specific to Zonguldak’s transition (e.g., land use changes, corporate-municipal interactions, socio-economic data from coal to gas economy). This will provide a robust, real-world case for testing the PACT framework.

Transmitting: I will focus on translating PACT's findings into effective outreach and educational materials, ensuring the co-produced knowledge reaches local governance, industry (especially in construction and energy), and the community to foster an inclusive and equitable transition.

scientificExpertise: Management of Technology and Innovation; Management Information Systems; Organization studies; Human resource management
scientificBackground: My core academic expertise in Management Information Systems (MIS), Organization Studies, and Management of Technology and Innovation provides the crucial analytical and managerial framework for PACT. My research emphasizes using Quantitative Methods and system design to manage complexity and enable strategic change across fragmented organizations, which directly mirrors the challenges of PCTs balancing global and local interests. I bring technical skills for data mining and analysis and the capacity to develop integrated information systems. This is essential for the Action’s goal of creating a holistic understanding and breaking down sectoral silos. I can help transform the fragmented knowledge and diverse data sets (land use, socio-economic, environmental) from the pilot studies into a systemic, visual apparatus for effective decision-making and collaborative planning.
motivation: I am motivated to join PACT to apply my strategic and organizational expertise to the urgent challenge of inclusive energy transition in Port City Territories. The Action's recognition that this transition requires more than a technological response—it demands a value-based, collaborative methodology—resonates strongly with my background. I aim to contribute by addressing the fragmentation of data and authority that hinders sustainable, just transitions. By participating in the co-creation of the PACT Framework (WG1) and its implementation in pilot studies (WG4), I want to help establish a replicable methodology that effectively engages disenfranchised local communities. This is a vital opportunity to leverage my organizational science skills to promote environmental justice and democratic participation in shaping Europe's energy future.
contribution:
I propose to contribute to Working Group 2 (WG2): Knowledge discovery in data. This group is central to reframing narratives by using spatial analysis and data visualization. My role will be to leverage my MIS and Quantitative Methods expertise to:

Systematize Data: Lead the collecting and collectively analyzing data sets (T2.1) from diverse disciplines and stakeholders, ensuring the information is usable for cross-national comparison.

Develop Tools: Identify and implement online open-access tools (T2.2) (like KUMU or Miro) to manage and visualize complex spatial, environmental, and socio-economic data, transforming raw information into shared knowledge.

Cross-WG Strategy: Support WG1 (PACT Framework) by structuring the process of knowledge co-production (D1.2), ensuring the framework is based on rigorous, multidisciplinary, and spatially integrated data, thus mitigating the high risks associated with data and trust in PCT governance (Risk 3).

scientificExpertise: Algorithms, distributed, parallel and network algorithms; Machine learning algorithms; Artificial intelligence, intelligent systems, multi agent systems
scientificBackground: Assoc. Prof. Dr. Ahmet Cevahir Çınar is a computer engineer and AI researcher at Selçuk University, and the founder of Sentius Technology, an R&D company located in Konya Technopark. His expertise includes artificial intelligence, optimization algorithms, machine learning, data analytics, and decision support systems for sustainable digital transformation. He has coordinated several national R&D projects funded by TÜBİTAK, TÜSEB, and KOSGEB, focusing on intelligent systems, environmental data processing, and smart monitoring solutions. His research integrates computational intelligence with participatory and data-driven frameworks, aiming to enhance transparency, resilience, and inclusiveness in technology-enabled transitions. He has published multiple Q1 journal papers and actively mentors interdisciplinary projects combining AI, sustainability, and stakeholder engagement in complex socio-technical systems.
motivation: My motivation to join the PACT Action stems from my interest in applying AI and data analytics to support inclusive and sustainable energy transitions in port city territories. I aim to contribute to understanding how digital tools, optimization models, and visualization platforms can strengthen stakeholder engagement and data-informed decision-making. The interdisciplinary and collaborative nature of PACT aligns with my work on AI-based monitoring and digital infrastructures for sustainability assessment. Participation in this Action will allow me to bridge computational models with social and environmental perspectives, contributing to cross-sector knowledge exchange and evidence-based policy innovation. I am particularly motivated to contribute to the creation of open, accessible digital tools that help local actors, researchers, and policymakers jointly envision pathways toward just and green transitions.
contribution: I plan to contribute primarily to WG2 (Knowledge Discovery in Data), WG4 (Pilots), and WG5 (Transmitting). My focus will be on developing AI-driven data analysis and visualization approaches to map and interpret energy transition processes in port city territories. I can support the creation of digital dashboards and interactive web tools that integrate environmental, social, and infrastructural datasets to inform pilot studies. Drawing from my experience in optimization algorithms and sustainability-focused analytics, I will also contribute to modelling energy efficiency scenarios and evaluating pilot outcomes. Furthermore, I will assist in disseminating project results through open-access digital resources, workshops, and policy briefs that communicate scientific findings to broader audiences, ensuring that data-driven insights become actionable knowledge for local communities and decision-makers.

scientificExpertise: Spatial development, land use, regional planning
scientificBackground: I have been researching the Tagus River waterfront for almost 2 years, as a permanent member of the Cidade Tejo research group, linked to IST and CITUA.
motivation: I am a PhD student at IST and a researcher of urban growth, with Lisbon being one of my case studies.
contribution: Until next year, I will continue as a researcher in this group and will participate in the PACT activity taking place in Lisbon in March 2026.

scientificExpertise: Sustainability
scientificBackground: An accomplished PhD with demonstrable experience in energy and environmental policy research, development and implementation underpinned by robust quantitative and economic analysis. Proven track record creating policy impact through applied sustainability and evidence-based research in an international context.

contribution: My contribution will be the coordination and transmission of outcomes and relevant information.

scientificExpertise: Mathematics applied to computer science, mathematical aspects of computer science; Sustainability for other engineering and technologies; Databases, data mining, data curation, computational modelling
scientificBackground: I am a Full Professor at İstanbul Medipol University with 15+ years of research at the intersection of decision sciences, energy transitions, and sustainability. My work advances cutting-edge multi-criteria decision making (MCDM), hybrid fuzzy models (q-ROF, IT2, hesitant 2-tuple), TOPSIS, VIKOR, DEMATEL, and AI-supported analytics to evaluate complex investments and policy trade-offs. Recent publications apply these methods to renewable energy, smart grids, hydrogen, carbon mitigation, and circular economy, demonstrating interdisciplinary integration across engineering, economics, and policy. Representative outputs include IEEE Access and Energy/Applied Energy–indexed studies on renewable energy project selection, micro-grid financing, and smart-grid drivers, plus an Elsevier book on renewable energy investments. This portfolio equips me to build comparable, transparent assessment frameworks for inclusive energy transition across diverse territories.
motivation: PACT’s aim, catalysing knowledge exchange for a holistic understanding of spatial and socio-cultural implications of energy transition in Port City Territories (PCTs), perfectly matches my research vision. I am motivated to join to (i) embed rigorous, explainable MCDM into PACT’s collaborative processes; (ii) help harmonise stakeholder visions and prevent marginalisation through transparent, value-based evaluation; and (iii) accelerate the transfer of best practices via open science. My experience building data-driven, participatory decision tools can operationalise PACT’s mapping, co-creating, structuring and transmitting objectives, while my editorial and project leadership background strengthens dissemination and capacity-building. I am particularly drawn to PACT’s commitment to inclusive engagement and FAIR, open-access outputs and to testing frameworks in real pilot territories.
contribution:
WG1/WG2 (Framework & Collaboration): Lead design of MCDM-based stakeholder and value-mapping (e.g., criterion co-creation, fairness/justice indicators). Deliver templates and workshops to break silos and structure multi-level governance debates; support D2.x deliverables.
WG3 (Knowledge Discovery/Mapping): Integrate open datasets into reproducible pipelines; apply spatially aware MCDM to reframe narratives and identify data gaps across disciplines and territories.
WG4 (Taxonomy/Glossary): Co-develop a shared taxonomy of transition criteria (technical, socio-cultural, environmental, financial) aligned with PCT contexts; link academic, professional and civic terms for consistent evaluation. 
WG5 (Pilots): Deploy and test decision tools in Le Havre, Livorno, Cádiz, and Rotterdam; run scenario thinking and back-casting with local stakeholders; synthesize transferable policy guidance. 
These contributions leverage my published MCDM toolkits for renewables/smart grids to produce actionable, open, and just transition pathways.

affiliation: Associacao ISCTE Conhecimento e Inovacao - Centro de Valorizacao e Transferencia de Tecnologias
scientificExpertise: Architecture and Urban Design
scientificBackground: I am an architect, academic and researcher with over 25 years of experience in waterfront transformation, climate resilience, and urban design. My PhD, Life and Death of Lisbon Waterfront, conducted in collaboration with the AA London, established the foundation of my long-term research on port-city relations. I coordinated the H2020 MSCA-RISE project SOS Climate Waterfront, involving different international partners and focused on resilience strategies for urban waterfronts. My scientific production includes edited books, peer-reviewed papers and keynote lectures on climate adaptation, sustainable urban environments, and design innovation. I have held academic positions at Université Laval (Canada), ULHT (Portugal) and have taught and lectured at institutions across Europe, North America, Asia, and Australia. My research expertise lies at the intersection of climate challenges, urban design experimentation, and collaborative methodologies, with a focus on real-world impact through pilot testing and knowledge transfer.
motivation: My motivation to join this COST Action is closely linked to my ongoing research and long-standing commitment to climate-resilient design strategies, particularly in coastal and waterfront contexts. Over the last decade I have collaborated with international teams to develop applied solutions that address climate risks while strengthening social and ecological systems. I see COST as a unique opportunity to expand this work through a multidisciplinary and open network that can mobilize knowledge beyond academic environments. I aim to contribute through expertise in design-driven research, and also to learn from ongoing initiatives across Europe. The shared objective of translating scientific insights into practical frameworks resonates with my experience leading international research projects and coordinating pilot-based experimentation. I am particularly interested in supporting dialogue between researchers, practitioners and local authorities in order to accelerate climate-responsive transformations.
contribution: As a member of WG4 – Pilots, I plan to contribute with my expertise in developing, coordinating and evaluating climate-adaptation pilot projects focused on urban and waterfront areas. I bring experience from the H2020 SOS Climate Waterfront project, where I led interdisciplinary teams and co-designed pilot interventions in diverse cities including Lisbon, Stockholm, Thessaloniki, and Rome. I can support the WG in defining methodological frameworks, selecting pilot territories, and establishing criteria to assess impact, transferability, and long-term resilience. I will also contribute to knowledge dissemination, including publications, public exhibitions, and collaborative workshops with municipalities and civil society. My role will include linking design research with implementation strategies, ensuring that pilots serve as demonstrators capable of generating replicable results. I also aim to promote exchange between partners through site-based learning, cross-case comparison, and international dialogue on climate-adaptive spatial transformation.

affiliation: Wroclaw University of Environmental and Life Sciences
scientificExpertise: Sustainability; Socio-economic aspects of environmental sciences; Quantitative methods for the social sciences
scientificBackground: My research background is in climate change, energy and environmental economics, with a strong focus on how environmental stress and energy systems shape social and economic outcomes. I have worked on issues such as deforestation, energy poverty, and environmental degradation, using empirical analysis to understand how energy access, land use, and environmental risks affect communities. This has given me a solid understanding of the challenges linked to energy transitions, especially in regions where environmental pressure and social vulnerability overlap. I am also interested in how inclusive planning and community involvement can support fair and sustainable transitions. My experience helps me connect environmental data, socio-economic impacts, and policy needs, which aligns well with PACT’s focus on inclusive energy transition, knowledge co-production, and the social and spatial dynamics of port city territories. I aim to contribute with an analytical, sustainability-focused perspective that bridges environmental and societal dimensions.
motivation: I want to join this COST Action because it connects strongly with my interest in climate change, energy transitions, and how these shifts affect people and places. Port city territories are at the center of major environmental and social challenges, and I am motivated to learn how different disciplines work together to design more inclusive and sustainable solutions. My work on energy poverty, environmental degradation, and climate impacts has shown me that technical change alone is not enough; communities need fair access, clear communication, and planning that considers social realities. PACT offers a chance to learn from real examples, such as the pilot studies in Le Havre, Livorno, Rotterdam, and Cadiz, and to understand how collaborative planning can shape better futures. I am motivated to contribute my analytical perspective while learning new methods of co-creation, mapping, and inclusive transition that can support fair energy policies.
contribution: I can contribute by bringing a climate and energy economics perspective to the social and spatial questions raised in this Action. In PACT Framework, I can support discussions on how environmental risk, energy access, and social vulnerability can be integrated into a shared model for inclusive energy transition in port city territories. In Knowledge Discovery in Data, I can help interpret environmental, socio-economic, and energy-related datasets, drawing on my experience with empirical analysis of climate impacts, deforestation, energy poverty, and degradation. For Theoretical Knowledge, I can contribute viewpoints on how energy transitions connect with fairness, community well-being, and long-term sustainability, linking economic principles with social justice goals. In Transmitting, I can assist in developing clear, accessible ways to communicate findings to communities, policymakers, and other stakeholders, ensuring that knowledge is shared in a way that supports understanding, trust, and practical action.

scientificExpertise: Artificial intelligence, intelligent systems, multi agent systems; Control engineering; Energy aspects of electrical and electronic engineering; Simulation engineering and modelling; Computational modelling and simulation
scientificBackground: Electrical and electronics engineering researcher focusing on renewable energy systems, electric mobility, and microgrid optimization. My current work integrates solar PV, wind power, hydrogen storage, and electric vehicle charging systems within university campuses and urban areas. I specialize in techno-economic modelling, computational optimization, and data-driven energy planning using tools such as HOMER, MATLAB, and AI-based algorithms. My recent studies investigate how electric vehicle charging infrastructure and hybrid renewable systems can support sustainable energy transitions aligned with city-scale strategies. These research activities overlap with port-city challenges where electrification, smart charging clusters, and hydrogen-based storage can serve maritime logistics, transport, and industrial areas. My academic experience provides a multi-disciplinary perspective connecting engineering, energy transition models, urban mobility, and data-driven planning relevant to the goals of PACT.
motivation: I am motivated to contribute to the PACT Action because port cities are becoming strategic experimental zones for energy transition, electrification, and sustainable mobility. My current research on renewable-based electric vehicle charging and hydrogen-integrated microgrids can be strongly aligned with the needs of port territories where heavy transportation, maritime logistics, public fleets, and industrial loads coexist. I aim to collaborate with experts from multiple disciplines to expand knowledge production on energy planning methods, policy implications, and techno-economic evaluation frameworks. Participating in this Working Group will support exchanging best practices, developing joint research publications, and exploring pilot applications with ports where mobility electrification and renewable integration can accelerate inclusive and just energy transitions. My objective is to bring data-driven modelling perspectives, while learning from socio-technical and policy-oriented experts to contribute to shared European strategies for sustainable port territories.
contribution: I plan to contribute by developing data-driven analyses, simulation models, and techno-economic evaluation tools that can support decision-making for energy transition in port territories. I can provide modelling of electric vehicle charging infrastructures, hydrogen storage systems, smart microgrids, and renewable integration scenarios to help identify cost-effective and socially inclusive pathways for port-city cooperation. I also aim to participate in joint publications, collaborative workshops, and pilot case assessments where engineering models can complement social, cultural, and governance-based approaches. I am willing to share modelling tools, datasets, and computational methods with WG members to co-produce knowledge that bridges digital planning, energy policy, and inclusive sustainability. Additionally, I am prepared to collaborate on comparative studies between different European ports, supporting reproducible methodologies and common evaluation metrics to guide future large-scale transitions.

scientificExpertise: Architecture and Urban Planning | Geoinformation and Spatial Data Analysis
scientificBackground: I am an Architecture and Urbanism student with an academic background focused on urban analysis, spatial planning, and territorial representation. I am currently involved in the CidadePortoTejo research project, which investigates the city–port relationship in Lisbon, with emphasis on the territorial, infrastructural, social, and landscape impacts of this interface. I have a strong interest in georeferencing tools, GIS, digital cartography, and spatial analysis, applied to the study of urban dynamics. My experience includes territorial data collection, production of thematic maps, critical reading of urban space, and support for the analysis of morphological and functional transformations in port and waterfront areas.
motivation: I am motivated to join this COST Action because of its strong potential to connect data, territory, and complex urban processes. My main interest lies in deepening the use of georeferenced data to understand urban systems, particularly in port areas, which are strategic territories undergoing constant transformation. My involvement in the CidadePortoTejo project strengthened my interest in the multiscalar dimension of city–port relations and in the use of data as a key tool for analysis, simulation, and decision-making support. Participating in this COST Action represents a valuable opportunity for interdisciplinary learning, international knowledge exchange, and the practical application of data-driven methods in the fields of architecture and urbanism.
contribution: I intend to contribute mainly to the Knowledge Discovery in Data and Pilots Working Groups, based on my background in Architecture and Urbanism and my involvement in the CidadePortoTejo research project. I can support the organization, analysis, and interpretation of spatial data, map production, information visualization, and participation in pilot studies focused on city–port relations and sensitive urban areas. I can also contribute with urban reading, morphological analysis, territorial diagnosis, and the integration of technical data with urban design and planning perspectives. I am interested in actively supporting collaborative activities, workshops, and the development of methodologies that integrate georeferencing, urban planning, and applied experimentation.

scientificExpertise: Product design, ergonomics, mechanical engineering aspects of man-machine interfaces; Energy
scientificBackground: I am an Associate Professor of Mechanical Engineering at Muş Alparslan University in Türkiye, specializing in sustainable and low-carbon energy systems. My research focuses on hydrogen and ammonia utilization, waste-to-energy conversion, and hybrid renewable systems that contribute to regional energy transition and decarbonization strategies. I have led and participated in national and international R&D projects involving industrial energy optimization, circular resource use, and environmental impact assessment. My current academic focus connects technological innovation with spatial and socio-economic transformation, aligning with PACT’s interdisciplinary approach to inclusive energy transition. By integrating engineering solutions with systemic, value-based, and participatory frameworks, my expertise supports the co-creation of sustainable strategies for energy hubs such as port and industrial city territories.
motivation: My motivation to join the COST Action PACT stems from my commitment to advancing sustainable and inclusive energy transitions through interdisciplinary collaboration. I believe that technological innovation alone cannot ensure a just and resilient energy future; it must be complemented by spatial, social, and cultural integration. The PACT framework offers a unique platform to exchange knowledge and co-create methodologies that connect engineering, environmental science, and urban transformation. By contributing my expertise in hydrogen-based and hybrid energy systems, I aim to help explore how port and industrial city territories can serve as living laboratories for decarbonization and community-oriented innovation. Participation in this Action will also enable me to build new research partnerships, strengthen transnational collaboration, and transfer best practices to developing regions facing similar transition challenges.
contribution: I plan to contribute to the Working Group by integrating my expertise in sustainable energy systems, hydrogen and ammonia utilization, and industrial decarbonization with PACT’s collaborative, spatial and socio-cultural framework. I aim to support the development of analytical tools for mapping energy flows, identifying transition barriers, and evaluating the environmental and socio-technical impacts of emerging energy strategies in port city territories. I will participate in co-creation workshops, contribute to methodological development for knowledge co-production, and help translate technical findings into inclusive planning approaches. Additionally, I intend to bring insight from industrial regions in Türkiye—particularly areas with port-like logistics, energy hubs, and vulnerable ecosystems—to enrich comparative case studies. By bridging engineering analysis with participatory and value-based perspectives, I aim to strengthen the Action’s capacity to design equitable and practical pathways for sustainable port city transitions.

scientificExpertise: Cultural Anthropology
scientificBackground: I am a cultural anthropologist with continuous training in anthropology at all academic levels, including a Bachelor’s and Master’s degree, followed by a PhD and postdoctoral research. I currently teach cultural anthropology in both the Bachelor’s and Master’s programmes in Anthropology at Leiden University. My research focuses on infrastructural development in port city territories and in hinterland regions connected to ports through infrastructure corridors. I have conducted long-term ethnographic fieldwork in China and Ghana, examining how infrastructure-led development shapes economic opportunities, labour regimes, social relations, and environmental conditions. My work analyses the everyday experiences of communities and workers involved in or affected by these large-scale projects, offering a grounded understanding of how global development models are implemented and negotiated locally. This perspective contributes to broader discussions on the societal and environmental implications of emerging infrastructural transformations.
motivation: My motivation to join this COST Action stems from the alignment between its aims and my current research agenda. I am particularly interested in "Port City Territories in Action: A Collaborative Laboratory for Inclusive Energy Transition (PACT)" because it provides a platform to examine how energy-related infrastructural projects reshape port cities and their connected hinterlands. As my work focuses on the social, economic, and environmental implications of infrastructure-led development, I see strong potential for contributing an ethnographic perspective to the Action. I am eager to collaborate with researchers from diverse disciplines to better understand how energy transitions unfold in practice. Participating in this network would also help me expand comparative insights across different port regions and strengthen interdisciplinary approaches to studying emerging energy infrastructures.
contribution: I plan to contribute actively to the Working Group. I am willing to take part in the organisation of workshops and conferences, as well as to present my ongoing research at COST Action-related events. I am also committed to co-writing papers and other joint publications. In addition, I aim to contribute to comparative analyses by engaging in collective reflections that bridge anthropology with other disciplines represented in the network.

scientificExpertise: Sustainability
scientificBackground: My name is Ali Abdelshafy, an Assistant Professor in Future Industrial Systems at TU Delft. I received my PhD from RWTH Aachen University and holds a Master’s degree in Resource and Environmental Management, as well as a B.Sc. in Petroleum Engineering. Before joining TU Delft, I led the research group “Climate-Neutral Industries” at the Chair of Operations Management at RWTH Aachen University. I was also a visiting fellow at the Oxford Institute for Energy Studies and the University of Cambridge. My research focuses on the sustainable transformation of industrial systems, with a focus on energy transition, industrial decarbonization, circular economy, mobility, and digitalization. My research focuses on improving modeling and simulation methods to evaluate trade-offs and enhance optimization of industrial transformation. I develop and use frameworks that integrate different methods and tools, such as material flow analysis, life cycle assessment, techno-economic evaluation, multi-criteria decision analysis, geographic information systems and optimization.
motivation: I am motivated to join PACT because my research focuses on the sustainable transformation of industrial and mobility systems, with a particular interest in energy transition, circularity, and the socio-technical implications of decarbonization. Port city territories are pivotal nodes in global value chains, industrial ecosystems, and energy systems, making them essential environments for shaping just and inclusive transitions. The Action’s emphasis on co-creation, spatial and socio-cultural dimensions, and value-based approaches complements my work integrating quantitative modeling with broader systemic perspectives. I am particularly drawn to the opportunity to collaborate with multidisciplinary experts and contribute to knowledge co-production that supports ports and cities in navigating complex transition challenges. Joining PACT will enable me to connect technical, economic, and spatial analyses with participatory and policy-oriented frameworks, helping to inform more resilient and inclusive port-city futures.
contribution: For WG2 (Knowledge Discovery in Data), I can contribute expertise in data-driven analysis, GIS-based assessments, material and energy flow modeling, and multi-criteria decision frameworks to map transition dynamics, spatial patterns, and industrial–urban interactions in port territories. In WG4 (Pilots), I can support methodological development and evaluation of the pilot sites by applying sustainability assessment, techno-economic evaluation, and systems modeling to understand transition challenges and opportunities in real port-city contexts, particularly Rotterdam. For WG5 (Transmitting), I will contribute to translating analytical insights into accessible decision-support materials, policy recommendations, and stakeholder engagement outputs. Drawing on my academic and applied experience, I can help synthesize findings, support capacity-building activities, and promote knowledge exchange between researchers, port authorities, and local communities.

scientificExpertise: Soil pollution; Waste treatment (environmental engineering); Environmental biotechnology, e.g. bioremediation, biodegradation; Bioreactors and applied microbiology for environmental engineering; Sustainability
scientificBackground: I am a soil and environmental microbiologist with more than ten years of research experience in bioremediation, soil health, and the sustainable valorization of contaminated soils and sediments. My work focuses on microbial processes, biosurfactant-producing bacteria, and bio-based technologies for removing organic pollutants and stabilizing heavy metals in soils and sediments. I have conducted postdoctoral research in France, China, Oman, and Pakistan, working on bioreactors, electrokinetic remediation, biosurfactants, microbial degradation pathways, and environmental geochemistry. I have contributed to multidisciplinary projects integrating soil microbiology, environmental engineering, and waste valorization, particularly targeting sustainable treatment of polluted matrices. I have authored more than 40 peer-reviewed publications, book chapters, and conference contributions in these areas. My scientific background closely aligns with the goals of this COST Action, especially regarding microbial-based remediation strategies, environmental risk reduction, and developing innovative approaches for sustainable reuse of contaminated resources.
motivation: I am highly motivated to join this COST Action because it aligns closely with my research interests in soil and sediment remediation, microbial technologies, and sustainable valorization of contaminated resources. Participating in this network will allow me to collaborate with leading European experts and contribute my experience in biosurfactant-based remediation, microbial degradation processes, and the geochemical assessment of polluted matrices. I am particularly interested in contributing to the development of harmonized methodologies, sharing experimental data, and participating in joint research activities that advance innovative approaches for risk reduction and resource recovery. Joining this Action represents an excellent opportunity to strengthen international collaborations, integrate my ongoing work in France and previous research across Europe and Asia, and contribute to multidisciplinary solutions for environmental challenges. I am enthusiastic about supporting training schools, workshops, and publications that enhance the scientific impact and practical implementation of sustainable remediation strategies.
contribution: I plan to contribute to the Working Groups by sharing my expertise in soil and sediment remediation, microbial biotechnology, and biosurfactant-based strategies for pollutant removal and stabilization. I can support the development of experimental protocols, data harmonization frameworks, and comparative assessments of bioremediation technologies across different environmental matrices. My research experience with electrokinetic remediation, microbial degradation pathways, and the valorization of contaminated sediments will help advance WG discussions on innovative and sustainable treatment approaches.
